brand.title: ESource AI Group
brand.subtitle: Knowledge Check — How to Use NotebookLM by Google

questions.question: What is NotebookLM best described as?
choices: A video editing tool
A source-grounded AI workspace
A file storage service
A general chatbot
questions.feedback_correct: Right — NotebookLM is a source-grounded workspace. The quality of its output depends on the sources you give it.
questions.feedback_incorrect: NotebookLM is a source-grounded AI workspace — unlike a general chatbot, it works from the sources YOU give it, and its output quality depends on those sources.

questions.question: Where do you go to create outputs like Video Overviews, Audio Overviews, Slide Decks, and Infographics?
choices: The source panel
The settings menu
Studio
The sharing screen
questions.feedback_correct: Correct — Studio is where all the output tools live.
questions.feedback_incorrect: The source panel holds your materials, but Studio is where you create outputs — Video Overviews, Audio Overviews, Slide Decks, and Infographics all live there.

questions.question: What do citations in NotebookLM help you do?
choices: Make the output longer
Verify where information came from
Share your notebook with others
Speed up generation
questions.feedback_correct: Right — citations point back to the source so you can check facts before using or sharing.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Citations are your fact-checking trail. They show exactly where in the source each claim came from, so you can verify before you share.

questions.question: What was the first artifact you created in this course, and from what kind of source?
choices: An infographic from a PDF
A slide deck from meeting notes
A video overview from a website URL
An audio overview from a spreadsheet
questions.feedback_correct: Correct — the first exercise was: add a website URL as a source, open Studio, and generate a Video Overview.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Think back to the first hands-on exercise: we added a website URL as a source, opened Studio, and generated a Video Overview. Source in, artifact out, citation check.

questions.question: Which type of source material works BEST for an infographic?
choices: A long personal essay
Steps, comparisons, statistics, or timelines
A single photograph
An audio recording
questions.feedback_correct: Right — infographics shine when the source has structure: steps, stats, comparisons, frameworks, or processes.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Infographics need structure to work with. Steps, comparisons, statistics, frameworks, and timelines translate into visuals; a long essay or a photo doesn't.

questions.question: Your generated output has become noisy and unfocused. According to the course, what should you try FIRST?
choices: Add more sources for context
Reduce the number of sources and regenerate
Switch to a different output type
Share it and fix it later
questions.feedback_correct: Correct — too many unrelated sources blur the output. Fewer, stronger sources bring back the focus.
questions.feedback_incorrect: More sources usually make noise worse, not better. The course rule: when output drifts or gets noisy, REDUCE the sources and regenerate.

questions.question: A teammate asks you to upload a confidential client contract to build a briefing. What is the right move?
choices: Upload it — NotebookLM is private by default
Upload it but turn on Chat View
Do not upload it unless it is approved for that use
Upload only the first half
questions.feedback_correct: Right — confidential and restricted material stays out unless it's approved for that use. You stay responsible for what you upload, generate, and share.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Speed never removes responsibility. Confidential, private, or restricted material stays out of NotebookLM unless it's approved for that use — no workaround setting changes that.

questions.question: You turned on Chat View before sharing a notebook. What is TRUE?
choices: Hidden content is now permanently deleted
Hiding content is not the same as revoking access
Only editors can see the notebook now
The link stops working for the public
questions.feedback_correct: Correct — Chat View hides content from that view, but people with access may still reach it. Hiding is not revoking.
questions.feedback_incorrect: This is the trap: Chat View only hides content from that view. People with access may still reach it. Hiding is not the same as revoking access.

questions.question: You must send a briefing to five named executives and no one else. Which sharing choice fits, and why?
choices: Public — it is fastest
Public with Chat View on — the content is hidden
Restricted — only invited people can access
Featured — it marks the notebook as important
questions.feedback_correct: Right — Restricted means only invited people get in. That's the fit when the audience is a named list.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Public means ANYONE with the link — including whoever it gets forwarded to. When only named people should see it, the answer is Restricted: only invited people can access.

questions.question: Before sharing any NotebookLM artifact at work, which set below matches the course's pre-share quality check?
choices: Audience clear, purpose clear, claims cited, titles clean, takeaways concise, rights confirmed
Spellcheck done, colors on brand, file under 10 MB, link tested
Sources deleted, chat cleared, notebook renamed, link copied
Video generated, audio generated, deck generated, infographic generated
questions.feedback_correct: Perfect — that's the checkpoint that protects your credibility before anything goes out.
questions.feedback_incorrect: The pre-share check is about credibility, not file mechanics: audience clear, purpose clear, claims cited, titles clean, takeaways concise, rights confirmed. Run it before anything goes out.
